subject: math
topic_title: Idea of Sets — Types, Notation and Operations
source_title: New General Mathematics SSS 1 by M.F. Macrae et al
learning_objectives: Define a set and describe sets by listing elements or by property
Use correct set notation including ∈, ⊂, ∪, ∩, ∅ and complement
Identify and distinguish types of sets: universal, finite, infinite, empty, disjoint, subsets
Perform set operations: union, intersection, and complement

sections.heading: Definition and Notation of Sets
sections.content: A set is a well-defined collection of objects or elements with a common characteristic. Sets can be described by listing elements (e.g., {1, 2, 3}) or by property (e.g., {x : x is an even number less than 10}). Key notations: ∈ means 'is an element of'; ∉ means 'is not an element of'; n(A) is the number of elements in A; ∅ or {} is the empty/null set; = means two sets are equal if they contain the same elements (repeated elements counted once).

sections.heading: Types of Sets
sections.content: Universal set (U or ε): a set containing all elements under consideration. Finite set: has a fixed number of elements, e.g., B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. Infinite set: has unending elements, e.g., D = {5, 10, 15, 20, ...}. Subset (⊂): B ⊂ A means all elements of B are in A. Empty set (∅): contains no elements. Disjoint sets: two sets with no elements in common, so P ∩ Q = ∅.

sections.heading: Set Operations
sections.content: Intersection (∩): A ∩ B contains elements common to both A and B. Union (∪): A ∪ B contains all elements from A and B combined. Complement (A'): elements in the universal set not in A. Examples: given U = {a,b,c,d,e,f}, P = {b,d,e}, Q = {b,c,e,f}: P ∩ Q = {b,e}; P ∪ Q = {b,c,d,e,f}; (P ∩ Q)' = {a,c,d,f}; (P ∪ Q)' = {a}; P' ∪ Q = {a,b,c,e,f}; Q' ∩ P' = {d}.
